whatToFeed: High-nitrogen liquid seaweed fertilizer.
pruningTips: Pinch off the center shoots regularly to prevent flowering and encourage bushy growth.
flavorProfile: Sweet, warm, and peppery with hints of anise.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Plant outdoors late May to early June, after soil reaches 70°F.
whenToHarvest: Continuous harvest once plant is 6-8 inches tall.
usesForLargeHarvests: Pesto, drying, infusing in olive oil, freezing in ice cube trays.
hardinessNote: Grow as annual in Zone 6/7
plantingInstructions: Sow seeds indoors 6 weeks before the last frost date, pressing 1/4 inch deep into fine seed-starting mix. Keep soil warm (70-75°F). Transplant outdoors in late May or early June once all danger of frost has passed, spacing plants 10-12 inches apart.
growthRequirements: Requires rich, loose, moist loam that drains exceptionally well, with a preferred pH of 6.0 to 7.5. Demands consistent, deep watering at the soil level rather than overhead, and a full day of unobstructed hot summer sun.

whatToFeed: Light, balanced organic liquid fertilizer.
pruningTips: Cut back leaves before flower buds open to maintain the best oil and flavor concentration.
flavorProfile: Sweet, mild, and herbaceous with subtle citrus and pine notes.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Start seeds indoors late March; transplant outdoors mid-to-late May after last frost.
whenToHarvest: Harvest leaves right as flower buds begin to form for peak flavor.
usesForLargeHarvests: Drying, herbed butter, infusing vinegars, seasoning blends.
hardinessNote: Grow as annual in Zone 6/7
plantingInstructions: Sow seeds indoors in late March. Broadcast tiny seeds on top of the soil and press down gently, as they require light to crack open. Transplant outdoors in mid-to-late May after all frost warnings clear, spacing them 8-10 inches apart.
growthRequirements: Thrives best in average, somewhat lean, sandy, well-drained soil with an alkaline pH between 6.5 and 7.5. Needs modest watering once established and maximum daily sun exposure to synthesize its delicate aromatic oils.

whatToFeed: Nitrogen-rich organic fish emulsion.
pruningTips: Harvest outer leaves frequently to delay bolting, especially during hot summer snaps.
flavorProfile: Bright, citrusy, and pungent, while seeds have a warm, spicy flavor.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Sow directly outdoors in mid-April for spring crop, or late August for autumn crop.
whenToHarvest: Cut external leaves when plant reaches 6 inches tall; harvest seed heads when they turn brown.
usesForLargeHarvests: Salsa, freezing in oil, grinding coriander seeds, chimichurri sauce.
hardinessNote: Grow as a cool-season annual in Zone 6/7
plantingInstructions: Directly sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in early spring (mid-April) where they are to grow, as taproots dislike transplanting. Space seeds 1-2 inches apart, and thin emerged seedlings to a final spacing of 6 inches.
growthRequirements: Prefers light, crumbly, loose loam with a pH of 6.2 to 6.8. Needs uniform moisture to slow down bolting. Thrives in bright sun during cool spring weather, but benefits heavily from partial afternoon shade as summer heat builds.

whatToFeed: Weak dilution of a balanced organic liquid feed.
pruningTips: Pinch the upper leaves to encourage bushiness unless you are intentionally growing them for seed heads.
flavorProfile: Tangy, bright, and distinctly feathery-green with a slight anise undertone.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Direct sow outdoors mid-to-late April; repeat every few weeks until mid-summer.
whenToHarvest: Harvest fresh fronds just before flowers open; gather seed heads when flat and brown.
usesForLargeHarvests: Pickling, dill butter, drying, tzatziki sauce, infusing vinegar.
hardinessNote: Grow as annual in Zone 6/7
plantingInstructions: Sow seeds directly outdoors 1/4 inch deep into highly prepared beds from mid-April onward. Space rows 12 inches apart and thin out sprouts to a final spacing of 8 inches. Sow fresh patches every 3 weeks for continuous leaf yield.
growthRequirements: Requires loose, well-draining, moderately fertile sandy-loam with an open pH range of 5.5 to 6.5. Must have complete full sun and protection from strong, buffeting winds due to tall, hollow, fragile stem structures.

whatToFeed: Light application of organic compost around the base in spring.
pruningTips: Prune woody stems back by a third in early spring to encourage fresh, flavorful leaf flushes.
flavorProfile: Earthy, musky, and warm with notes of pine and citrus.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Plant rooted cuttings or transplants outdoors in late April to early May.
whenToHarvest: Harvest lightly in the first year; thereafter, pick leaves continuously spring through autumn.
usesForLargeHarvests: Drying, brown butter sage sauce, sage stuffing blends, freezing leaves in olive oil.
hardinessNote: Hardy perennial in Zone 6/7
plantingInstructions: Plant purchased nursery starts or rooted cuttings outdoors in late April or early May, spacing them 18-24 inches apart. Firm soil gently around the root crown and water carefully to minimize immediate shock.
growthRequirements: Demands dry, light, gravelly, or sandy loam soils with exceptionally sharp drainage and a pH of 6.5 to 7.0. Highly drought-tolerant once roots run deep; overwatering will kill roots quickly. Requires broad, full sun.

whatToFeed: Fish emulsion or nitrogen-rich liquid fertilizer.
pruningTips: Cut stems at the base of the plant, starting with the outermost older leaves.
flavorProfile: Clean, bright, and robustly herbaceous.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Direct sow or plant transplants outdoors in mid-to-late April.
whenToHarvest: Harvest stems from the outside of the cluster continuously throughout the season.
usesForLargeHarvests: Tabbouleh, gremolata, chimichurri, freezing in cubes, drying.
hardinessNote: Biennial grown as an annual in Zone 6/7
plantingInstructions: Soak tough seeds in warm water for 24 hours before planting to boost germination speeds. Sow outdoors 1/4 inch deep in mid-April, spacing rows 12 inches apart. Thin emerging clumps to a final spacing of 6 inches.
growthRequirements: Requires deeply cultivated, rich, moist, humus-laden soil that retains moisture but drains clean, with a pH of 6.0 to 7.0. Thrives in full sun to partial afternoon shadow spots.

whatToFeed: Balanced organic liquid kelp fertilizer.
pruningTips: Remove any yellowing lower leaves to keep the core cluster productive.
flavorProfile: Mild, grassy, and subtly crisp texture.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Direct sow or plant transplants outdoors in mid-to-late April.
whenToHarvest: Cut individual stems at ground level once the plant has at least three segments.
usesForLargeHarvests: Garnishes, herb bouquets, freezing in cubes, drying, soup bases.
hardinessNote: Biennial grown as an annual in Zone 6/7
plantingInstructions: Start indoors in peat pots 6-8 weeks before frost, or sow directly outside in late April after soaking seeds overnight. Plant 1/4 inch deep and space out seedlings to 6-8 inches apart upon settlement.
growthRequirements: Thrives in evenly moist, fertile garden loams rich in rotted compost, maintaining a stable pH range of 6.0 to 6.8. Needs consistent watering intervals to keep tight leaf clusters from crisping up. Full sun to part shade.

whatToFeed: Light application of balanced organic compost tea.
pruningTips: Cut back by half just before flowering to stimulate a fresh flush of flavorful leaves.
flavorProfile: Bold, earthy, and intensely aromatic with a slight bitterness.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Set out transplants in late April or early May after danger of heavy frost.
whenToHarvest: Clip stems throughout summer, maximizing harvest right before the flowers open.
usesForLargeHarvests: Drying, Italian/Mexican seasoning blends, infusing olive oil, tomato sauces.
hardinessNote: Hardy perennial in Zone 6/7
plantingInstructions: Set container transplants into dry spring beds around early May, giving them a 12-inch clear radius to spread out. Press soil over root balls firmly and apply minimal watering to encourage outward rooting.
growthRequirements: Demands lean, fast-draining, gravelly or rocky soils with a pH of 6.5 to 8.0. Naturally adapted to dry conditions; can easily tolerate long periods without rainfall. Must have complete, uninterrupted full sun exposure.

whatToFeed: Minimal feeding required; light kelp meal in spring.
pruningTips: Prune lightly after flowering to maintain bush shape and prevent the center from turning woody.
flavorProfile: Woody, aromatic, and pungent with pine and lemon notes.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Plant established potted transplants outdoors in mid-to-late May.
whenToHarvest: Clip fresh sprigs continuously throughout the spring, summer, and autumn.
usesForLargeHarvests: Drying sprigs, rosemary salt, herb-infused oils, roasting with potatoes.
hardinessNote: Perennial; may need winter protection or indoor overwintering in Zone 6
plantingInstructions: Plant vigorous nursery starts outdoors in mid-to-late May once regional frost risks dissolve. Space individual bushes 2 feet apart, or put in containers that can easily move inside when cold elements shift in November.
growthRequirements: Demands hyper-porous, gritty sandy-loam structures with a neutral to alkaline pH of 6.5 to 7.5. Must completely dry out between watering cycles. Needs all-day scorching full sun; highly susceptible to mildew in humid or soggy soil.

whatToFeed: Very light compost layer in early spring.
pruningTips: Cut back the tips regularly to promote fresh shoots; avoid cutting into old wood.
flavorProfile: Earthy, floral, and slightly sweet with a subtle clover essence.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Plant transplants outdoors in late April to early May.
whenToHarvest: Clip sprigs as needed; main harvest is best just before summer flowers open.
usesForLargeHarvests: Drying, bouquet garni, thyme honey, infusing vinegars, herbed salt.
hardinessNote: Hardy perennial in Zone 6/7
plantingInstructions: Set potted starts out in late April, spacing individual plants 8-12 inches apart. Clear away heavy clods of dirt and set the root collar level with the terrain line, watering minimally until new green leaflets pop.
growthRequirements: Requires sandy, gravelly, poor quality soils with unmatched drainage and an alkaline pH of 6.5 to 8.0. Thrives on neglect and intense full sun. Roots will rot or suffer freeze damage if standing water pools over winter.

whatToFeed: Avoid standard fertilizers; loves lean, sandy alkaline soil additions.
pruningTips: Prune back by one-third after flowering in late summer, but never cut into bare wood.
flavorProfile: Floral, sweet, and highly aromatic with subtle pine and rosemary undertones.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Plant rooted transplants outdoors in May into well-draining, sandy soil.
whenToHarvest: Harvest flower spikes in mid-summer when the lowest blossoms on the stem begin to open.
usesForLargeHarvests: Dried sachets, essential oil extraction, lavender syrup, baking/culinary uses.
hardinessNote: Hardy perennial in Zone 6/7
plantingInstructions: Plant established nursery container sets outdoors in May. Space plants 18 inches apart to encourage ample airflow between branches. Mix grit or perlite into the planting hole to maximize immediate drainage.
growthRequirements: Demands lean, nutrient-poor, rocky or sandy soil with top-tier drainage performance and a strict pH of 6.7 to 7.3. Requires absolute full sun. Wet roots or dense clay will kill the plant during summer high humidity or freezing winter drops.

whatToFeed: Minimal feeding required; light compost application in spring.
pruningTips: Prune back explicitly in mid-summer to encourage new, soft green growth.
flavorProfile: Bittersweet, cooling anise and sweet licorice notes.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Plant young transplants outdoors in late April to early May.
whenToHarvest: Harvest fresh leaf tips continuously from June until frost.
usesForLargeHarvests: Tarragon vinegar, Béarnaise sauce bases, freezing in oil, drying.
hardinessNote: Hardy perennial in Zone 6/7
plantingInstructions: Must be cultivated from rooted cuttings or nursery plant divisions, as French tarragon does not produce viable seeds. Space starts 18 inches apart in late April or early May when spring turns mild.
growthRequirements: Prefers light, sandy loam soil with clean, rapid drainage and a neutral pH of 6.5 to 7.0. Needs moderate watering intervals; leaves will drop or lose flavor if roots stay soggy. Flourishes best under full sun.

whatToFeed: Balanced 5-5-5 organic fertilizer early in the spring.
pruningTips: Harvest by cutting leaves from the outside of the clump down to 2 inches above the soil line.
flavorProfile: Mild, delicate onion flavor.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Direct sow or plant divisions outdoors in late March to early April.
whenToHarvest: Cut blades from spring to autumn once the plant is at least 6 inches tall.
usesForLargeHarvests: Freezing in ice cube trays, chive butter, chive blossom vinegar, drying.
hardinessNote: Hardy perennial in Zone 6/7
plantingInstructions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep outdoors in early April, or plant clump divisions sourced from an established patch. Space clumps or thin seedlings to 6-8 inches apart in rows. Water thoroughly until established.
growthRequirements: Thrives in rich, high-humus, well-draining garden soil with a preferred pH of 6.0 to 7.0. Requires consistent, moderate moisture to sustain steady green blade growth. Adapts fine to full sun or partial shade plots.

whatToFeed: Slow-release granular nitrogen fertilizer.
pruningTips: Deadhead the white flower heads before they drop seeds to prevent aggressive spreading.
flavorProfile: Distinct, sweet garlic flavor with fresh herbal undertones.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Plant seeds or divisions outdoors in April.
whenToHarvest: Harvest flat green blades continuously from late spring until winter dormancy.
usesForLargeHarvests: Stir-fries, dumplings, forgetting, chive oil, fermenting.
hardinessNote: Hardy perennial in Zone 6/7; spreads readily via seed
plantingInstructions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in spring or tuck division groupings 6 inches apart inside garden beds. Keep the soil evenly moist during initial root growth phases. Once anchored, they spread reliably via small tuberous roots.
growthRequirements: Requires fertile, loam soil with high organic levels and a pH profile of 6.2 to 7.0. Needs steady moisture parameters, yet demands clean drainage to stay disease-free. Prefers full sun locations to foster strong flat blades.

whatToFeed: Balanced organic liquid plant food once a month.
pruningTips: Cut back aggressively to control spread. Grow in containers to prevent runner invasion.
flavorProfile: Sweet, cooling, and intensely refreshing without being sharp.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Plant container transplants outdoors in late April or May.
whenToHarvest: Harvest leaves or sprigs continuously throughout spring, summer, and autumn.
usesForLargeHarvests: Mint jelly, drying for tea, mint syrups, freezing in water cubes, mojitos.
hardinessNote: Hardy perennial in Zone 6/7; highly invasive if uncontained
plantingInstructions: Highly recommended to drop individual nursery transplants into large, deep plastic containers to isolate aggressive underground runners. If planting in-ground, drop 15 inches apart in isolated landscape spaces.
growthRequirements: Demands continuously moist, rich, organic, and loose loam soils with a wide pH tolerances from 6.0 to 7.5. Performs beautifully in partial shade locations, though it handles full sun perfectly if root zones remain wet.

whatToFeed: Rich organic compost or standard fish emulsion.
pruningTips: Keep runners trimmed back tightly. Pinch tops down regularly to stimulate lower leaf density.
flavorProfile: Sharp, intensely cool, and peppery mint flavor due to high menthol content.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Plant container transplants outdoors in late April or May.
whenToHarvest: Pick leaves regularly; harvest heavily just before the plant blossoms.
usesForLargeHarvests: Peppermint tea drying, extract making, syrups, mint pestos.
hardinessNote: Hardy perennial in Zone 6/7; highly invasive if uncontained
plantingInstructions: Tuck potted root slips or starters into separate patio containers or raised beds lined with thick barrier mesh in late April. Space multiple starts 12-18 inches apart if using open, demarcated garden spots.
growthRequirements: Thrives in deep, rich, wet clay-loam soils heavily saturated with compost material, keeping a pH of 6.0 to 7.0. Requires frequent watering intervals to avoid leaf wilt. Prefers partial shade but treats full sun.

whatToFeed: Diluted organic nitrogen fertilizer in spring and mid-summer.
pruningTips: Prune back to the ground before winter; keep contained in a pot to stop runners.
flavorProfile: Clean mint flavor reminiscent of thin-mint candy with a distinct cocoa nuance.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Plant container transplants outdoors in May.
whenToHarvest: Harvest sprigs or leaves continuously from late spring through late autumn.
usesForLargeHarvests: Dessert garnishes, infusing syrups, drying for tea, baking extracts.
hardinessNote: Hardy perennial in Zone 6/7; highly invasive if uncontained
plantingInstructions: Set out potted transplants in May. To lock down aggressive creeping habits, submerge a bottomless 5-gallon bucket into the earth and plant the mint directly inside the bucket's rim to cut off rogue root runners.
growthRequirements: Prefers exceptionally damp, nutrient-dense loam soil structures holding a neutral pH of 6.5 to 7.0. Requires high hydration input levels. Grows rapidly under standard full sun or dappled light conditions.

whatToFeed: Light balanced organic granular fertilizer.
pruningTips: Cut back by half after flowering to stop self-seeding and refresh woody foliage.
flavorProfile: Sweet, bright lemon flavor with mild minty undertones.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Direct sow or plant transplants outdoors in mid-to-late April.
whenToHarvest: Clip leaves or whole stems continuously throughout the season as desired.
usesForLargeHarvests: Drying for hot tea, herbal syrups, infusing liquors, insect repellent sprays.
hardinessNote: Hardy perennial in Zone 6/7; aggressive self-seeder if not deadheaded
plantingInstructions: Sow seeds directly outdoors in late April, barely dusting them with soil as they need light to sprout. Alternatively, plant nursery starts spaced 15-18 inches apart to allow for its robust, bushy mature mounding size.
growthRequirements: Grows vigorously in almost any well-drained soil profile, preferring fresh sandy-loam with a pH of 6.0 to 7.0. Requires regular moisture but can survive short dry spells. Thrives in full sun or light shade.

whatToFeed: Avoid heavy fertilizers; thrives on a thin layer of organic compost.
pruningTips: Harvest the daisy-like flower heads regularly to encourage continuous blooming.
flavorProfile: Appley, sweet, and deeply soothing with straw-like herbal accents.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Direct sow outdoors in early-to-mid April (seeds require light to germinate).
whenToHarvest: Harvest flower heads on a sunny morning when the petals are fully expanded.
usesForLargeHarvests: Drying flowers for sleep tea, chamomile salves, hair rinses.
hardinessNote: Grow as annual in Zone 6/7; readily self-seeds for next year
plantingInstructions: Broadcast seeds directly over weed-free soil surfaces in mid-April. Press them down firmly into the ground with flat palms without burying them, as light exposure triggers germination. Thin out sprouts to 6 inches apart.
growthRequirements: Prefers poor, sandy, light-loam soils with decent drainage and a pH range of 5.6 to 7.5. Too much fertilizer will result in lots of feathery leaves but very few blossoms. Demands open, intense full sun.

whatToFeed: Organic liquid fertilizer high in potassium to promote blue blooms.
pruningTips: Pinch back young plant tips to make them stockier; deadhead flowers to limit aggressive seeding.
flavorProfile: Fresh, crisp, and clean flavor exactly like a cucumber.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Direct sow seeds outdoors from mid-April to May.
whenToHarvest: Harvest young tender leaves before hairs become stiff; harvest flowers when fully open.
usesForLargeHarvests: Candied flowers, freezing blossoms in ice cubes, adding leaves to salads/drinks.
hardinessNote: Grow as annual in Zone 6/7; prolific self-seeder
plantingInstructions: Directly sow large seeds outdoors 1/2 inch deep in mid-spring, spacing seeds 4-6 inches apart. Once seedlings establish, thin them out to a wide 12-to-15-inch final spacing, as taproots grow rapidly and break if moved.
growthRequirements: Thrives in ordinary, loose, dry-to-medium garden soils with excellent internal drainage and a pH range of 6.0 to 7.0. Tolerates poor, lean terrain well. Thrives in full sun but adapts nicely to half-shade setups.

whatToFeed: Minimal fertilizer needed; a simple organic compost tea works best.
pruningTips: Shear back by half after the first bloom cycle to spark a second wave of dense foliage.
flavorProfile: Pungent, woody, and slightly skunky mint flavor.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Direct sow or plant transplants outdoors in mid-to-late April.
whenToHarvest: Cut full stems down late in summer when the plant is in full flower.
usesForLargeHarvests: Drying for cat toys, calming herbal teas, natural pest-deterrent infusions.
hardinessNote: Hardy perennial in Zone 6/7
plantingInstructions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep indoors in early spring, or plant nursery starts directly in late April. Space individual bushes 15-18 inches apart in a perennial frame or a container to control its spreading roots.
growthRequirements: Thrives in completely average, slightly dry, sandy or gritty soils with a broad pH range of 6.0 to 7.5. Drought-resistant once anchored. Requires sharp drainage and grows best under a full day of bright sun.

whatToFeed: Light application of balanced organic liquid fertilizer monthly during summer.
pruningTips: Pinch back the growing tips in early summer to promote bushier growth and prevent the plant from becoming too leggy.
flavorProfile: Sweet, strong licorice and anise flavor, slightly more robust and sweet than French tarragon with a hint of warm tarragon spice.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Plant outdoors late May to early June, after all danger of frost has passed and soil is warm.
whenToHarvest: Harvest leaves continuously throughout summer; pick golden yellow edible flowers in late summer and autumn.
usesForLargeHarvests: Tarragon vinegar, drying for herbal teas, seasoning chicken and fish dishes, infusing honey.
hardinessNote: Grow as annual in Zone 6/7; handles summer heat much better than French tarragon
plantingInstructions: Sow seeds indoors 6-8 weeks before the last frost, or set purchased transplants out in late May once temperatures are warm. Space individual plants 12 inches apart, firmly tamping down the soil around the base.
growthRequirements: Demands well-drained, moderately fertile loam or sandy-loam soil with a preferred pH of 6.5 to 7.5. Highly heat and drought-tolerant compared to French tarragon. Requires full sun to generate prolific autumn yellow flowers.
